stories.title: Why Your Home's Electricity Bill Is About to Go Up
stories.headline_kid: From July to September, Singapore families will pay about 17% more for electricity — because of a conflict happening far away in the Middle East.
summary: Singapore's Energy Market Authority (EMA) has announced that electricity prices for homes will rise by about 17% from July to September 2026. This 'tariff' is the price you pay for every unit of electricity your home uses, and it is reviewed every three months.

Why the sudden jump? Singapore does not have its own oil or gas fields, so almost all of our electricity is made using natural gas bought from other countries. A conflict in the Middle East has pushed up the price of natural gas around the world, and that cost has now reached our homes here in Singapore.

Think of it like the vegetables at the wet market. If a big storm damages farms overseas, vegetable prices here can go up too, because we buy from those farms. Electricity works the same way: it is switched on in Singapore, but the fuel that powers it often comes from far away.

The good news is that prices are reviewed every quarter, so if gas prices calm down, tariffs can come down too. In the meantime, EMA says households can look at different electricity price plans, and simple habits like switching off lights and unused appliances can help keep bills lower.

lenses.me: You might notice your parents reminding you to switch off the lights, fan or air-con more often this school holiday.
lenses.family: Your family's monthly electricity bill could be a bit higher for the next three months, so your parents might plan the budget more carefully.
lenses.friends: Almost every family in Singapore will see the same increase, so it could be a good topic for a class discussion on saving energy.
lenses.nation: Because Singapore imports almost all its energy, this shows how our small island nation is connected to big events happening in other parts of the world.
lenses.world: It's a reminder that a conflict thousands of kilometres away can still change the price of everyday things in Singapore, like electricity.

compo_words.meaning: the price charged for a service, like electricity or water
compo_words.example: The new electricity tariff meant our family's bill was higher this month.

compo_words.meaning: to keep changing, sometimes going up and sometimes down
compo_words.example: Prices of natural gas can fluctuate depending on world events.

compo_words.meaning: to bring in goods from another country
compo_words.example: Singapore has to import almost all of its natural gas from overseas.

compo_words.meaning: a serious disagreement or fighting between groups
compo_words.example: The conflict in the Middle East caused fuel prices to rise around the world.

compo_words.meaning: extremely important and needed
compo_words.example: Electricity is an essential part of daily life, powering our lights, fans and fridges.

compo_words.meaning: to control something using rules
compo_words.example: The Energy Market Authority helps regulate how much companies can charge for electricity.

compo_phrases.meaning: the amount of money needed to pay for daily needs like food, transport and bills
compo_phrases.example: Rising electricity prices are one part of the higher cost of living in Singapore.

compo_phrases.meaning: to spend less money and be more careful with spending
compo_phrases.example: With bills going up, many families have had to tighten their belts a little.

compo_phrases.meaning: when one event causes many other things to change, like ripples spreading in a pond
compo_phrases.example: The conflict overseas created a ripple effect that reached all the way to our electricity bills.

questions: Describe one way your family could save electricity at home. Why do you think this would help?
Do you think it is fair that Singapore's electricity prices depend on events happening in other countries? Why or why not?
How would you feel if your parents asked you to use the air-con less to save money? What would you do?
phrases: One thing we could do is ...

stories.title: Sentosa Is Getting a Super Makeover (With Nintendo World!)
stories.headline_kid: Sentosa Island is getting a huge 20-year makeover, with a floating boardwalk, refreshed beaches — and Singapore's very own Super Nintendo World.
summary: On 3 July 2026, the Sentosa Development Corporation (SDC) revealed its big new plan for the future, called the Greater Sentosa Master Plan. It covers Sentosa Island and the nearby island of Brani, and the changes will roll out over the next 20 years.

One of the most exciting parts for kids: an immersive Super Nintendo World is coming to Universal Studios Singapore! There are also plans for a brand-new zone called Brani West, which will be one of the largest areas for new attractions.

Sentosa's three beaches — Siloso, Palawan and Tanjong — will be refreshed, and a floating boardwalk will connect the smaller islets nearby, so visitors can go 'islet-hopping'. SDC hopes all these changes will help Sentosa welcome twice as many visitors as it does now — it already welcomed more than 16 million visitors in a single year.

Before building anything, SDC wants to hear from Singaporeans first. A travelling exhibition is visiting malls like VivoCity, Our Tampines Hub, Jurong Point and Waterway Point, so families can see the plans and share their own ideas for Sentosa's future.

lenses.me: One day, you might get to explore a real-life Super Nintendo World right here in Singapore, with games and characters brought to life.
lenses.family: Future family outings to Sentosa could look very different, with new beaches, boardwalks and attractions to explore together.
lenses.friends: You and your friends could start planning which new Sentosa attraction you want to try first when it opens.
lenses.nation: This shows how Singapore keeps reinventing itself to stay an exciting place to live in and visit, even though we are a small island.
lenses.world: Singapore is trying to become a world-class holiday destination that can attract visitors from all over the globe.

compo_words.meaning: to change something completely
compo_words.example: The master plan will transform Sentosa over the next 20 years.

compo_words.example: SDC unveiled its new plan for Sentosa at an exhibition.

compo_words.example: The new floating boardwalk will be a popular attraction for tourists.

compo_words.meaning: to make something feel fresh and new again
compo_words.example: The beaches at Sentosa will be rejuvenated as part of the plan.

compo_words.meaning: related to the area near the sea
compo_words.example: New coastal protection measures will help protect Sentosa's beaches.

compo_phrases.meaning: something that is expected to happen soon
compo_phrases.example: Exciting changes are on the horizon for Sentosa Island.
SDC's Brani West plans sound larger than life, with huge new attractions.

compo_phrases.meaning: to attract a lot of attention or excitement
compo_phrases.example: Super Nintendo World is expected to make a splash when it opens.

stories.title: Team Singapore Flies the Flag at the Commonwealth Games
stories.headline_kid: 53 Singaporean athletes are heading to Glasgow, Scotland, to compete against the best in the world at the 2026 Commonwealth Games!
summary: On 6 July 2026, Team Singapore had a special send-off ceremony at Eden Hall, ahead of the Glasgow 2026 Commonwealth Games. The Games will take place from 23 July to 2 August in Glasgow, Scotland, and Singapore is sending a contingent of 53 athletes — 22 women and 31 men — who will compete in seven sports, including swimming, athletics, gymnastics, basketball, cycling, bowls and weightlifting.

Singapore has actually taken part in every single Commonwealth Games since 1950! At the last Games in Birmingham in 2022, our athletes did brilliantly, winning 12 medals in total — 4 gold, 4 silver and 4 bronze.

In the pool, Singapore is sending six swimmers, led by national record holder Teong Tzen Wei, who won silver in the men's 50m butterfly at the last Games. Swimmer Gan Ching Hwee has been chosen as one of Team Singapore's flag bearers, and she says leading the team out is a huge honour, especially since this will be her very first Commonwealth Games.
Swimming events run from 24 to 29 July, so keep an eye out and cheer on our athletes as they represent Singapore on the world stage!

lenses.me: You could watch the swimming or athletics events and pick a Singapore athlete to cheer for, just like a mini World Cup!
lenses.family: Your family might gather in front of the TV together to watch Team Singapore compete, which can be a fun bonding activity.
lenses.friends: You and your classmates could talk about which sport or athlete you're most excited to watch.
lenses.nation: Team Singapore competing on the world stage brings the whole country together in national pride, even though we are a small country.
lenses.world: The Commonwealth Games bring together athletes from more than 70 countries and territories, celebrating friendship and fair competition across the globe.

compo_phrases.meaning: in front of an international audience

stories.title: A Company's Big Birthday Gift to Singapore Students
stories.headline_kid: To celebrate turning 45 years old, a Singapore company is giving $3 million over three years to help students who need a little extra support.
summary: On 14 July 2026, the founder of local steelworks company TTJ Design and Engineering pledged $3 million over the next three years to The Straits Times School Pocket Money Fund (STSPMF). The gift marks the company's 45th anniversary.

The STSPMF is a charity that gives out monthly pocket money to primary and secondary school students from families who need a bit more support. This money can help pay for things like transport to school, meals, and school supplies, so students can focus on learning without worrying about money.

Choosing to give during a happy milestone, like a company's birthday, is a lovely way to share good fortune with others. It shows how businesses in Singapore can play a part in looking after the community, not just making money for themselves.
Small acts of kindness — whether it's a big company donating millions, or you sharing your snack with a classmate — all add up to make Singapore a warmer, more caring place.

lenses.me: It might make you think about how you use your own pocket money, and how you could help a friend in need.
lenses.family: Your family might talk about the different ways people and companies in Singapore give back to those who need help.
lenses.friends: Some classmates you know might actually receive support from funds like this — a good reminder to always be kind and never judge.
lenses.nation: This shows Singapore's spirit of giving, where successful companies often choose to support less fortunate members of society.
lenses.world: Acts of generosity like this can inspire other companies and even other countries to support children's education.

compo_words.example: The company pledged $3 million to help students in need.

compo_words.meaning: to give something, especially money, to help others

stories.title: Oops! Bus Arrival Timings Went Missing for a Morning
stories.headline_kid: On the morning of 14 July, the system that tells you 'how many minutes more' for your bus stopped working properly — but don't worry, the buses themselves were running just fine!
summary: On the morning of 14 July 2026, the system that shows the expected arrival times of public buses was disrupted. The Land Transport Authority (LTA) sent out a notice through the MyTransport.SG app at 6.15am to let commuters know.

The important thing to remember is that bus services themselves were operating normally the whole time. It was only the digital countdown — the part that tells you 'your bus is arriving in 3 minutes' — that was affected, not the actual buses on the road.

Many families in Singapore, including students heading to school, rely on apps or bus stop screens each morning to plan their trip and avoid waiting too long in the hot sun or sudden rain. When these systems have a hiccup, it can feel confusing, but it doesn't mean transport has stopped working.

If this ever happens to you, it's good to have a backup plan: check the physical timing panel at the bus stop, ask the bus captain, or simply be patient, since LTA's engineers usually get these systems back up and running quickly.

lenses.me: You might have had to wait a little longer without knowing exactly when your bus to school would arrive that morning.
lenses.family: Parents checking the app before leaving the house might have felt a little more anxious than usual about being on time.
lenses.friends: You and your classmates might have swapped stories about waiting at the bus stop that morning — a fun, relatable icebreaker.
lenses.nation: It shows how much Singapore relies on smart technology for daily life, and also how our core services stayed reliable even when the tech had a glitch.
lenses.world: Cities all over the world depend on similar smart transport apps, and small tech hiccups like this happen everywhere — good backup plans matter.

compo_words.meaning: to interrupt or stop something from continuing as normal
compo_words.example: A technical fault disrupted the bus arrival timing system that morning.

compo_words.meaning: to inform someone officially about something
compo_words.example: LTA notified commuters through the MyTransport.SG app.

compo_words.meaning: able to be trusted to work well

compo_words.meaning: arriving or happening at the expected time
compo_words.example: Despite the glitch, most buses remained punctual that morning.

compo_words.meaning: a small problem that causes trouble or difficulty
compo_words.example: The system hiccup was only a minor inconvenience for commuters.

compo_phrases.meaning: returning to normal after a problem
compo_phrases.example: The bus arrival system was soon back on track after the morning hiccup.

compo_phrases.meaning: to stay calm and continue with what you were doing despite a problem

Usually, Singapore's bus system runs as smooth as clockwork.
